notes.note: Ante diem quartum Kalendas Iunias = 29 May 54 BC, the date Cicero arrived back at Rome from his Cumae stay.
The Latin colony on the Po (modern Piacenza), Quintus's last stop before crossing into Cisalpine Gaul on his way to Caesar's headquarters.
The quadrigae poeticae, the chariot-of-the-Muses figure adapted by Cicero into a metaphor of catching up: he will hurry his cultivation of Caesar by writing a poem on the British expedition.

notes.anchor: your colours and my own brush
notes.note: The painter's analogy: Quintus, eyewitness in Caesar's camp, supplies the colour; Cicero, at Rome, supplies the brush, the poet's craft.

A formal recognizance to appear in court on a stated day; the bedrock of Roman civil procedure. Caesar's joke is that in his entire entourage in Gaul there was no one who knew how to draft one until Trebatius arrived.
L. Domitius Ahenobarbus, consul 54 BC, the optimate stalwart whose lifelong feud with Caesar would close at Pharsalus; in office he made a parade of refusing to grant petty favours.

Ap. Claudius Pulcher, consul 54 BC, Domitius's colleague; his run on Caesar in Cisalpine Gaul that year is the joke about ``going to fetch some tribunate.'' Cicero will conclude an open accommodation with Appius later in 54.

notes.anchor: softer than the lowest little ear-tip
notes.note: Oricula infima molliorem: a Roman household proverb for extreme pliancy. Cicero promises Quintus that, both in public and in personal feuds, he will be a model of softness --- the post-Luca posture given private form.
Talk of an extraordinary dictatorship to break the year's electoral logjam --- talk that would intermittently surface from 54 BC through Pompey's sole consulship of 52 and onward.
